title: Agent CI Onboarding Tour
description: Explore the architecture of Agent CI, a local GitHub Actions runner built for AI coding agents. Learn how the CLI, mock API server, and website work together.

steps.title: Welcome to Agent CI
steps.description: Agent CI is a monorepo with two published packages and a marketing website. The top-level layout follows a standard pnpm workspace: packages holds the core libraries, apps holds the website, and experiments contains proofs-of-concept. Configuration and shared tooling live at the root.

interactiveActions.description: Hover over packages to see the two core packages
Click package.json to see the monorepo scripts

steps.title: CLI Package: The Heart of Agent CI
steps.description: The CLI package is the main entry point and what users install to run GitHub Actions locally. The highlighted files handle command parsing for run, retry, and abort, environment and repo detection, and core type definitions.

interactiveActions.description: Click the main executable to see the command orchestration

steps.title: Runner: Job Execution Engine
steps.description: This is where jobs actually execute. The core orchestrator (highlighted in red) manages Docker containers, step execution, and pause-on-failure. Supporting modules (highlighted in yellow) handle git command interception to work against a local working tree, workspace management, retry synchronization, and job metadata.

interactiveActions.description: Click the core orchestrator to see the largest file in the codebase

steps.title: Workflow Parsing & Docker Management
steps.description: Two critical subsystems sit alongside the runner. The workflow layer (in purple) parses GitHub Actions YAML, resolves job dependencies via topological sort, and expands matrix strategies. The Docker layer (in cyan) manages container lifecycle: configuration, service containers like databases, and cleanup on shutdown.

interactiveActions.description: Hover over the workflow layer to see the parser and scheduler
Hover over the Docker layer to see container management

steps.title: Terminal UI & Output System
steps.description: This layer handles everything the user sees in the terminal. The highlighted files draw the live UI, manage in-memory run state, and provide a quiet mode for AI agents. Other modules in this directory handle concurrency limiting, structured logging, and summary reporting.

interactiveActions.description: Click the agent mode file to see how AI agent output works

steps.title: DTU: The Mock GitHub API Server
steps.description: The Digital Twin Universe is a standalone HTTP server that emulates GitHub's API surface. It handles cache storage via local bind-mounts for near-instant caching, artifact upload and download, and GitHub API stubs. The highlighted server core (in orange) manages startup and in-memory state, while the routes layer (in pink) organizes dedicated handlers for caching, artifacts, and GitHub endpoints.

interactiveActions.description: Hover over the routes layer to see the cache, artifacts, and GitHub API handlers

steps.title: Marketing Website
steps.description: The marketing website is a React 19 app deployed to Cloudflare Workers. It features a hero section with terminal emulator demos, a compatibility matrix showing supported GitHub Actions features, and a panel-based layout built with Tailwind CSS.

interactiveActions.description: Click the landing page to see the home content

steps.title: CI/CD & Release Pipeline
steps.description: The project dogfoods itself with GitHub Actions workflows (highlighted in red). One workflow runs typechecking and tests, another does end-to-end validation covering matrix strategies, caching, artifacts, and services, and a third uses changesets for automated versioning and npm publishing. Every PR requires a changeset (highlighted in green) to keep the two packages versioned together.

interactiveActions.description: Hover over the workflows directory to see all CI pipelines

steps.title: The Full Picture
steps.description: You've now seen the complete Agent CI architecture: the CLI parses workflows and orchestrates Docker containers, the DTU server emulates GitHub's API for caching and artifacts, and the website markets the product. Data flows from workflow definitions through the parser, into the job scheduler, through Docker execution with the DTU providing API emulation, and back out through the terminal renderer. Happy contributing!

interactiveActions.description: Explore the codebase freely - click any building to dive deeper!
